title: Jargon lookup
text:
The nuggets try to gloss terms as they go. This page is the quick lookup: one plain sentence each, with a link to the nugget where the idea is developed.
RAG (retrieval-augmented generation): the common setup: upload documents, retrieve matching chunks at query time, generate an answer. The model rediscovers knowledge on every query; nothing accumulates. → Karpathy’s llm-wiki
llm-wiki: instead of retrieving from raw files, an LLM builds and maintains a wiki as it works, so knowledge compounds. → Karpathy’s llm-wiki
Front matter / typed edges: the small YAML block at the top of each page (type:, up:, extends:, supports:, criticizes:, related:) that records how pages relate. → Wiki front matter
RDF triple: a fact stored as subject-predicate-object, like this page criticizes that page, in a form a database can query. → The wiki is a knowledge graph
Reify: to turn something implicit (a typed edge in prose) into an explicit data record (an RDF triple). → Wiki front matter
SPARQL: the query language for RDF graphs; the graph equivalent of SQL. → The wiki is a knowledge graph
SHACL: a rule language that checks a graph’s structure is valid (for example, every supports: must cite a source). → The wiki is a knowledge graph
Ontology: an explicit statement of what the entities are, how they relate, and what the fields mean. Kept deliberately small here. → Ontologies, kept small
Random-Walk-With-Restart (RWR): a graph-ranking method that spreads importance across a graph from a set of starting nodes; the idea behind PageRank. → The wiki is a knowledge graph
Knowledge bundle: a wiki authored so that other people’s LLMs can consume it; the shareable unit of the ecosystem. → Knowledge bundles
OKF (Open Knowledge Format): Google Cloud’s open specification (2026) that formalizes the llm-wiki pattern as a portable bundle of markdown with YAML front matter, so wikis by different producers work with different agents. → Knowledge bundles
Agent card: a small public profile a group’s agent publishes: what it knows about and how to reach it. → Agent federation
ask primitive: the federation’s query channel: find which group has relevant knowledge, then query it (with permission). → Agent federation
MCP (Model Context Protocol): an open standard that lets any AI host (Claude Desktop, Cursor) use a shared tool or read shared data. → Agent federation
Connector: an agent that wraps a data source, declares a capability, does the interpretive extraction, and files results with provenance. → Connectors are agents
Marketplace: the distribution-and-discovery layer for installable tool plugins, the way federation is for bundles. → A marketplace for tools
Broker pattern: a non-LLM component holds the credential and returns only data, so the agent never sees the secret. → ControlMaster
ControlMaster: an SSH feature that authenticates a connection once and reuses it; a working instance of the broker pattern. → ControlMaster
Data fabric: an AI-driven integration layer that connects scattered data sources into one queryable, data-agnostic environment. → Data fabrics
FAIR: data that is Findable, Accessible, Interoperable, and Reusable; the data-management standard funders increasingly require. → Data fabrics
DID (decentralized identifier): a persistent identifier its subject controls cryptographically, resolvable without any central registry. → Decentralized identifiers
did:web: the simplest DID method: the identity document is just a file on a normal web domain, no blockchain. → Decentralized identifiers
Verifiable credential: a cryptographically signed claim, like a digital ID card, checked against a DID. → Decentralized identifiers

title: WGRA Nuggets
text:
One idea per nugget: short, self-contained, and browsable in any order. The flyer is the full pitch and the deep dives go long; these sit in between, a set of concepts you can graze rather than a story you follow start to finish.
Start anywhere. If you want the lineage, Karpathy’s llm-wiki is where the whole thing begins.









Decentralized identifiers



identity



Portable, self-sovereign identity so a source or agent is found and trusted without a central registry, the piece that keeps discovery open while access stays gated.





Jul 18, 2026







Data fabrics



ecosystem



A data fabric is a metadata-driven layer that unifies distributed data where it lives; our knowledge graph and connectors are what let a wiki-grounded agent plug into one.





Jul 14, 2026







ControlMaster: the broker pattern, already running



infrastructure



SSH connection multiplexing is a working, in-house example of the one rule for reaching credentialed sources: the agent never touches the secret.





Jul 9, 2026







Connectors are agents



ecosystem



A connector is not a static adapter but an agent that wraps a source, declares a capability, and files results with provenance; a small ontology per source makes it reliable.





Jul 2, 2026







A marketplace for tools



ecosystem



Some capabilities are plugins an agent installs, distributed through a marketplace the center can host, and discovery hides which is which.





Jun 27, 2026







Agent federation



ecosystem



Each group runs its own wiki and publishes an agent card; your agent can query a peer group’s memory, with permission, cited back to their pages.





Jun 19, 2026







Knowledge bundles



bundles



A wiki authored to be consumed by other people’s LLMs: portable, shareable compiled competence, with an OKF import/export path for interoperability.





Jun 11, 2026







The deep dives



deep-dives



Five longer treatments on the WGRA site: memory that compounds, team scale, agent capability, the knowledge graph, and knowledge bundles.





Jun 4, 2026







Our recipe: a collaborative llm-wiki



foundations



The llm-wiki-memory-template turns Karpathy’s single-user pattern into shared, attributed, multi-writer memory for a research group.





May 29, 2026







Ontologies, kept small



graph



A small, shared ontology is what lets an agent reason over described data instead of guessing, and the reliability comes from keeping it lightweight, not heavy.





May 21, 2026







The wiki is a knowledge graph



graph



Every typed edge reifies to an RDF triple, so the wiki is a SPARQL-queryable graph, which buys queries prose and grep cannot answer.





May 14, 2026







Wiki front matter: the typed edges



foundations

graph



A few lines of YAML at the top of each page carry the relationships that turn linked markdown into something an agent can reason over.





May 6, 2026







Karpathy’s llm-wiki



foundations



The pattern underneath everything here: an LLM that builds and maintains a wiki as it works, so knowledge compounds instead of being rediscovered on every query.

title: The wiki is a knowledge graph
text:
A knowledge graph represents information as entities (the nodes) and the relationships between them (the edges), with enough formal meaning attached that software can query and reason over it, not just display it. The infoboxes behind Wikipedia, feeding a graph you can query, are the familiar example. The wiki here becomes one almost for free.
Every typed edge in a page’s front matter reifies to (is recorded as) an RDF triple, a subject-predicate-object fact a database can query. So the wiki is not just interlinked markdown; it is a queryable knowledge graph, with a formal ontology (an explicit vocabulary of page and relationship types), SHACL rules that check the structure is valid, and a shipped library of SPARQL queries (SPARQL is the query language for graphs, like SQL for tables) an agent can run against the graph directly.
That layer buys queries prose and keyword search cannot answer. Ask which pages hold the wiki together: one query returned a page on Random-Walk-With-Restart (a graph-ranking method, the idea behind PageRank) with 48 inbound edges on a live research wiki. Walk a criticizes: edge transitively to surface every downstream claim a retraction implicates. Lint, at commit time, for supports: claims with no ingested source. These are graph queries over meaning, not keyword queries.
The distinction matters because a browsable pile of markdown limits an agent to what it can grep. A reified graph lets it query relationships, transitive closures, and structural roles. And it ships with the template, so a project gets it without building graph infrastructure.
One honest caveat is worth keeping. The graph is only as valuable as its consumption, and today an LLM navigating a wiki reads by name-matching, not by walking the graph. So the graph is dark matter until a retrieval step reads it on the LLM’s behalf. Building the graph is necessary; wiring it into the read path is the open work.

title: The wiki is a knowledge graph
text:
Related

Wiki front matter, where the triples come from.
Ontologies, kept small, the vocabulary and the validation rules.
Data fabrics, where standards-based graphs plug into the wider world.

title: Our recipe: a collaborative llm-wiki
text:
Karpathy’s pattern is written for one person and their agent. Our recipe, the llm-wiki-memory-template, is what it takes to make it work for a group.
The core is the same: an LLM-authored wiki of cross-linked markdown, front matter carrying typed edges, the agent doing all the maintenance. What we add is everything the group case needs.
Attribution. Every entry carries a by: <human> via <agent> line, so who claimed what, with which tool, stays legible, and git preserves the full amendment history.
A conflict protocol. When two collaborators write at once, mechanical conflicts resolve automatically and semantic ones route through an LLM-assisted merge, so both contributions land and neither overwrites the other.
Discipline in the artifact. A verification gate blocks numerical claims not backed by real output, and named discipline gates catch the rationalizations that produce bad writes. The discipline lives in the repo, not in whoever happened to prompt well.
It is also model-neutral. The wiki is the durable memory; the model doing the work today (Claude Code, Cursor, whatever comes next) is disposable. Different assistants read the wiki through their own commands and skills while sharing the same policy files, gates, and schema, so one student can use Claude Code and another Cursor on the same project, and adopting a better model next year is a new overlay, not a migration.
The recipe is a template, not a platform. You fork it, you own it, and you take your memory with you.

title: Our recipe: a collaborative llm-wiki
text:
Related

Karpathy’s llm-wiki, the single-user pattern this extends.
Agent federation, how one group’s wiki reaches another’s.
The deep dives, team scale and agent capability in full.

title: ControlMaster: the broker pattern, already running
text:
Reaching a credentialed source safely comes down to one rule: the LLM never touches the secret. A non-LLM broker holds the credential, makes the authenticated request, and returns only data. The agent reasons and asks for a result; it never sees the key.
We already run an instance of this, and it is not exotic: SSH ControlMaster. Accessing Notre Dame’s CRC uses connection multiplexing, where one authenticated connection is reused for many operations. An initial master connection authenticates once (SSH key plus a Duo two-factor prompt), and every downstream operation reuses the authenticated control socket without re-authenticating. Map it onto the rule and it lines up: the socket is the broker; the agent or skill talks to the socket, never the key or the 2FA; the human authorizes the master once; killing it revokes.
It is a coarse broker, honestly. A ControlMaster session carries full shell privilege, not a narrowly scoped permission, and it is campus-local, another group cannot use your socket. But it demonstrates the load-bearing architecture: authenticate once, out of band, and let the agent use a delegated channel. And it is forward-compatible: the same broker seam that holds an SSH key or a login token today can present a verifiable credential (a cryptographically signed claim, like a digital ID card) tomorrow.
The lesson: you reach credentialed data by putting a broker between the agent and the secret, and you already do this for the cluster.

title: ControlMaster: the broker pattern, already running
text:
Related

Decentralized identifiers, the portable credential the broker could carry next.
Connectors are agents, the things that reach credentialed sources.

title: Knowledge bundles
text:
A knowledge bundle is a wiki authored with the intent that other people consume it through their own LLMs. Bundles are wikis, but not every wiki is a bundle; the distinguishing property is intent to share and be queried by non-authors.
The point of a bundle is that it ships compiled competence, not just searchable content. A student learning knowledge engineering opens a query session against a bundle on that domain and gets the group’s accumulated judgment, not a reading list. Bundles come in two shapes: a reference bundle uses a neutral retrieve-synthesize-cite protocol; a pedagogical bundle ships a Socratic or worked-example protocol embedded in its own content, so the teaching travels with the material.
Because a bundle is just a git repo of markdown plus its graph, it is portable: you can take it to another tool. The roadmap adds import and export in OKF, the Open Knowledge Format, Google Cloud’s 2026 open spec that formalizes exactly this llm-wiki pattern as a portable markdown bundle, so our bundles interoperate with other open-knowledge tooling rather than living in a walled garden. That portability is the whole point: your memory is yours, and it moves.
This is the shareable unit of the ecosystem: the thing a group publishes and a peer consumes.

title: Knowledge bundles
text:
Related

Agent federation, how a bundle gets discovered and queried across groups.
Our recipe: a collaborative llm-wiki, what a bundle is made of.
A marketplace for tools, the sibling distribution channel for tools.

title: Decentralized identifiers
text:
Today discovery in the federation leans on a curated index, and access leans on a GitHub credential. Both work, and both tie identity to one platform’s account. Decentralized identifiers (DIDs) are how identity becomes portable.
A DID is a persistent, location-independent identifier that its subject controls cryptographically, resolving to a small document with no central registration authority (no company or registry has to vouch for it). It does three jobs discovery needs. It gives every source, agent, and bundle a stable identifier that survives moving hosts (the FAIR “findable” persistent-identifier principle, done without a central issuer). Its service endpoints are themselves a discovery primitive: resolve one DID and you get pointers to the source’s catalog, its agent card, its endpoints. And it anchors trust, a verifiable credential (a cryptographically signed claim, like a digital ID card) checked against a DID is how consumption gets gated while discovery stays open.
There is a neat fit with the rest of the stack: a DID document is itself RDF, so identity, description, and discovery live in one graph rather than three systems, and a DID is the canonical identifier a source’s small ontology attaches to. It is also the fourth leg of “locked in on nothing”: portable agent, portable memory, portable tools, and now portable identity, replacing the current reliance on a platform login.
The honest status: this is design and a plan, not something built. The cheapest test is did:web (the simplest DID method, where the identity document is just a file on a normal web domain, no blockchain) on the existing GitHub Pages, to prove portability before spending anything heavier.

title: Decentralized identifiers
text:
Related

Data fabrics, where FAIR and portable identity meet.
Agent federation, the discovery layer DIDs would open up.
ControlMaster, the credential seam a DID would slot into.

title: Ontologies, kept small
text:
The wiki’s graph is grounded in a formal ontology, the la3d/llm-wiki-colab vocabulary, with SHACL constraints (rules that check the structure is valid) over it. An ontology is just an explicit statement of what the entities are, how they relate, and what the fields mean.
The load-bearing idea is that the ontology should be small. An LLM reasoning over an explicit small ontology is accurate; an LLM inferring structure from raw, unlabelled data is measurably less accurate and often needs a human in the loop to catch mistakes. So the reliable path is not “make the agent clever enough to guess at undescribed sources.” It is the reverse: give every source a small ontology, cheap to author, and let the agent reason over that.
This is why the ontology stays lightweight rather than a heavy formal one that over-commits provisional structure. A small semantic layer is what makes reasoning trustworthy; a heavy one manufactures crispness and returns over-confident results.
It is also the connector contract. A source is not integrated when you can call its API; it is integrated when it is described well enough for an agent to reason over it without guessing. The same move applies to documents (a document-structure ontology improves extraction) and to any API or database a connector reaches. The small ontology is the reliability mechanism.

title: Ontologies, kept small
text:
Related

The wiki is a knowledge graph, what the ontology structures.
Connectors are agents, where a per-source ontology earns its keep.
Wiki front matter, the typed edges the ontology names.

title: Connectors are agents
text:
Off-the-shelf sources (papers, PDFs, notes, tables) come into the wiki through the standard ingest path. Databases, APIs, and complex figures need a connector, and a connector is not a static adapter.
Extraction is interpretive work: reading a table out of a paper, reconciling a supplementary spreadsheet against the paper’s notation, normalizing to a schema, attaching provenance and a confidence, retrying when a source is malformed. That is open-ended, so a connector is an agent that wraps a source, declares a capability, does the messy extraction, and files the result with its provenance. The researcher’s hub agent discovers and orchestrates a fleet of them.
The reliability mechanism is a small ontology per source (see Ontologies, kept small): reasoning over an explicit small ontology is accurate, guessing at raw responses is not. So a connector is finished not when it can call the API but when the source is described well enough to reason over without guessing.
We built one against Notre Dame’s PAD service: it reads the service’s own ontology and its OpenAPI spec (a machine-readable list of the API’s calls), exposes one generic read-only tool, and answers live questions with no per-endpoint code and no interface at all (see it working). Discovery abstracts over delivery: the agent asks for a capability and does not care whether it resolves to a plugin it installs or a service it calls.

title: Connectors are agents
text:
Related

Ontologies, kept small, the per-source description that makes a connector reliable.
A marketplace for tools, the other way a capability reaches an agent.
Agent federation, where extracted knowledge gets shared.

title: Karpathy’s llm-wiki
text:
Most work with LLMs and documents is RAG (retrieval-augmented generation): upload files, retrieve matching chunks at query time, generate an answer. It works, but the model rediscovers the same knowledge from scratch on every question. Nothing accumulates.
Andrej Karpathy’s llm-wiki pattern flips that. Instead of retrieving from raw documents at query time, the LLM incrementally builds and maintains a persistent wiki: a structured, interlinked set of markdown pages that sits between you and the sources. Add a new source and the model reads it, extracts what matters, and integrates it, updating entity pages, revising summaries, flagging where new data contradicts old claims. The knowledge is compiled once and kept current, not re-derived per query.
The key property is that the wiki is a compounding artifact. The cross-references are already there, the contradictions already flagged, the synthesis already reflects everything read. You never write it; the LLM does the bookkeeping (summarizing, cross-referencing, filing) that makes a knowledge base useful over time. You curate sources and ask questions; the LLM does the grunt work. Obsidian is the IDE, the LLM is the programmer, the wiki is the codebase.
The lineage runs back to Vannevar Bush’s Memex (1945): a private, curated knowledge store with associative trails between documents. Bush’s vision was closer to this than to what the web became. The part he could not solve was who does the maintenance: humans abandon wikis because the upkeep grows faster than the value. The LLM does not get bored, does not forget a cross-reference, and can touch fifteen files in one pass, so the wiki stays maintained because maintenance costs almost nothing.
Everything else in these nuggets is what happens when you take this pattern seriously for a research group.

title: Karpathy’s llm-wiki
text:
Related

Our recipe: a collaborative llm-wiki, the same pattern made multi-writer.
Wiki front matter, the metadata that gives the pages structure.
The deep dives, the longer treatments on the main site.

title: A marketplace for tools
text:
Not every tool is a connector that reaches out to a source. A graph extractor or a figure reader is a capability an agent simply installs. Those travel through a marketplace.
The marketplace is the distribution-and-discovery layer for tools, the way federation is for bundles. A researcher’s agent discovers a capability (say “graph extraction”) and installs it, and the center can host the marketplace cheaply. Crucially, discovery abstracts over delivery: the agent finds a capability and uses it, without caring whether that resolves to a plugin it installs from the marketplace or a connector it calls over the network. Delivery mode is an operations decision, never a user-facing one.
This is not hypothetical. A working plugin marketplace already packages group skills into installable plugins, validated end to end with the Claude Code command-line tool. So the marketplace leg of the ecosystem is proven, not promised; what remains is a connector-specific kit on top of it.
Together with connectors and federation, the marketplace is how the group’s tools stop being buried in one pipeline and become resources any researcher’s agent can find.

title: A marketplace for tools
text:
Related

Connectors are agents, the sibling delivery mode (reach out vs install).
Agent federation, the same discover-then-use idea for knowledge.

title: Data fabrics
text:
What a data fabric actually is. A data fabric is a design for data management, not a product: an integrated layer (the “fabric”) that sits over an organization’s scattered, heterogeneous data sources and makes them usable together. Gartner’s framing is that it uses metadata to automate the integration work teams otherwise do by hand. The defining move is that it connects data where it lives rather than copying everything into one store: centralized in access, but not necessarily consolidated in storage.
Three ingredients make that work. Active metadata: not just static schemas and glossaries (that is passive metadata) but continuous signals about how data is actually used, which the fabric analyzes to suggest and automate connections. A knowledge graph and ontology: the semantic layer that links raw sources to shared concepts, so a query can span systems by meaning instead of by hand-wired joins. And AI: to discover assets, infer relationships, and keep the integration current as sources change.
That is what separates a fabric from the things it gets confused with. A data lake or warehouse consolidates: move all the data into one big store first. A portal wires one bespoke integration per source. A fabric does neither; it leaves data in place and unifies it through the metadata and semantic layer. (A data mesh, for contrast, is a governance stance, distributed domain ownership, and can be built on fabric technology.)
Why a wiki-grounded agent belongs on this picture. The pieces a fabric is built from are pieces we already have. The wiki’s knowledge graph and small ontologies are a semantic layer. The typed edges are active metadata. Connectors are the automated integration. So a group’s outputs come out as standards-based RDF, ready to plug into a wider institutional fabric, and FAIR by construction on three of four axes. The fabric is the how of connecting data at scale; the wiki-grounded agent is how an individual researcher participates in one, and keeps the judgment and provenance a fabric alone does not carry. (The picture we mean.)

title: Data fabrics
text:
Related

The wiki is a knowledge graph, the semantic layer a fabric runs on.
Connectors are agents, the automated integration into the fabric.
Decentralized identifiers, portable identity and the FAIR “findable” anchor.

title: The deep dives
text:
The nuggets here are bites. When you want the full argument on one of the pillars, the WGRA site has a deep dive for each, longer, worked, with real examples.
These nuggets and those deep dives are two lengths of the same story: graze here, go deep there.

title: The deep dives
text:
Related

Karpathy’s llm-wiki, the idea the deep dives build on.
Our recipe: a collaborative llm-wiki, the implementation behind them.

title: Wiki front matter: the typed edges
text:
Every page in the wiki opens with a small block of YAML front matter. Two fields are required: type: (what kind of page this is) and up: (its parent). The interesting part is the optional typed edges: extends:, supports:, criticizes:, requires:, related:. Each names a relationship to another page, by name.
This is the load-bearing metadata. related: is an honest catch-all for provisional structure; extends:, supports:, and criticizes: are stronger claims, used where the material actually makes them. A page that criticizes another says so in one line, and that line is machine-readable.
Front matter does three jobs at once. It places the page in a hierarchy that humans and agents navigate by. It records the relationships between ideas that prose otherwise buries in sentences. And it reifies to a graph: reify just means turning that implicit relationship into an explicit data record, so every typed edge becomes an RDF triple (a subject-predicate-object fact, like this page criticizes that one, that a database can query).
The discipline is calibrated to content. An experimental wiki keeps structural placement strict and treats related: as a catch-all; a review wiki over a fixed corpus can afford aggressive typed edges. Forcing crisp edges onto provisional work manufactures a confidence the material does not have.
The point: the metadata is cheap to write, the agent maintains it as it works, and it is the seam where a pile of markdown becomes a graph.

title: Wiki front matter: the typed edges
text:
Related

The wiki is a knowledge graph, what those triples let you query.
Ontologies, kept small, the vocabulary the edges draw from.
Karpathy’s llm-wiki, the pattern these pages instantiate.

title: Agent federation
text:
Federation means independent systems that each keep their own data, control, and rules, while agreeing on a shared way to interoperate: no central authority owns the whole. Email federates (many servers, one protocol); so does the web. Agent federation applies that shape to research-group memory.
The problem it solves: every research group is quietly re-answering questions another campus group answered last year, and when a PI leaves, that group’s institutional memory often walks out with them.
Each group runs its own Wiki-Grounded Research Agent: its own wiki, its own memory, its own permissions. Its agent publishes an agent card, a small public profile that declares what the group knows about and how to reach it. Your agent can then query a peer’s memory, their benchmark results, their tried-and-abandoned methods, the workflow that produced a specific figure, with the peer’s permission, and cited back to specific pages in their wiki.
The consumer channel is an ask primitive: discover which group has relevant knowledge, then query it. Discovery is open (anyone can find that a bundle exists); consumption is gated (the peer’s repository permissions decide who reads). A read-only MCP server (Model Context Protocol, an open standard that lets any AI host such as Claude Desktop or Cursor use a shared tool) lets those hosts reach shared bundles too, a route separate from ask.
Federation keeps memory where it belongs, in each group, while making it reachable. Institutional knowledge stops being locked in individual labs and becomes your peers’ actual work, queryable through your agent.

title: Agent federation
text:
Related

Knowledge bundles, the thing a peer publishes and you query.
Connectors are agents, how outside sources get into a wiki in the first place.
Decentralized identifiers, portable identity for who is allowed to read.

title: About WGRA Nuggets
text:
WGRA (“Wiki-Grounded Research Agent”) is a pattern for research-group memory: an LLM-authored wiki that sits between a group’s raw sources and its AI agent, as durable, shared, git-versioned project memory.
These Nuggets break the idea into its conceptual parts, one bite each. They are deliberately short and independent, meant for browsing, not reading in order. The main WGRA site carries the full flyer and the longer deep dives; the nuggets are the graze-able middle.
Maintained by Chris Sweet, Center for Research Computing, University of Notre Dame (csweet1@nd.edu).
